# Prefer Clear Ownership

**Flexibility: Required (MUST)**

## The Principle

Every engineering artifact — service, repository, pipeline, database,
API, shared library, infrastructure component — MUST have a designated
owner who is accountable for its health, compliance, and evolution.
Ownership MUST be recorded in a machine-readable location and kept
current.

## Concept Foundation

- **JOS-C03 (Boundary Management)** — Boundaries without owners are
  boundaries without enforcement. Ownership assigns accountability to
  a specific boundary, ensuring that someone is responsible for what
  crosses it, what stays inside it, and what happens when it is violated.
- **JOS-R03 (Ownership Required — meta layer)** — The meta-layer rule
  establishes that every governed component must have an owner. This
  eng-domain principle applies that requirement concretely to engineering
  artifacts: codebases, services, infrastructure, and data stores.

## Why This Matters

Unowned artifacts decay. Without a named owner, no one triages the
alerts, no one reviews the dependency updates, no one decides when to
deprecate. Shared ownership in practice means no ownership — when
everyone is responsible, no one is. Clear ownership creates a single
point of accountability: one person or team who can answer "what is
the state of this system?" at any moment. This does not mean the owner
does all the work; it means the owner ensures the work gets done.

## Tension and Tradeoffs

Strict ownership can create silos. If only the owning team may touch a
service, cross-cutting improvements stall. The principle addresses
accountability, not access control — contributors may come from any team,
but the owner remains the accountable party. There is also the problem
of orphaned artifacts: when an owner leaves or a team is reorganized,
ownership must be explicitly transferred, not silently abandoned. The
cost of ownership transfer is real but far smaller than the cost of
unowned infrastructure.

## 1970/2070 Note

In 1970, ownership was implicit — the person who wrote the program owned
it. In 2070, AI agents may author and maintain systems autonomously, but
accountability still requires a human (or a governed entity) in the loop.
The need for someone to answer "who is responsible?" is permanent.

## Baseline+Overlay Note

This eng-domain principle extends JOS's universal governance. It operates
in addition to, not instead of, meta-layer principles. Specifically, it
provides the engineering-concrete interpretation of JOS-R03 (Ownership
Required) from the meta layer.
